title: The Capital Picture Hiding in Your EOFY Balance Sheet
linkedin:
Most business owners open their EOFY accounts for the tax conversation and set them aside. But the balance sheet is the most complete capital structure snapshot the business will produce all year.

Here is the read most owners skip.

The current ratio (current assets divided by current liabilities) tells you whether the business can meet its short-term obligations from what it already holds — or whether July's commitments are being funded by revenue that hasn't arrived yet. An ATO liability on a payment plan sits in current liabilities and reduces this ratio directly, which matters before any bank conversation in the new year.

Net debt divided by EBITDA is what a commercial lender uses to assess borrowing capacity. Most banks are comfortable extending facilities where this ratio sits below 2.5. Above 3.0, unsecured lending becomes difficult regardless of current trading performance. Director loan balances affect this calculation in ways most owners don't realise until they are in the conversation.

Retained earnings sit in the equity section and signal something to any lender who reads them: that the business generates consistent profit and manages its capital with enough discipline to leave some inside the structure. A business that distributes everything it earns has thin equity and typically requires property security or personal guarantees for new facilities — even where trading is strong.

With June 30 eleven days away, the accounts being finalised now contain this picture in full. Reading them for capital before filing them away takes less time than most owners expect and answers the questions that tend to come up in August when it's too late to change the starting position.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
The EOFY balance sheet tends to get reviewed for the tax outcome and then filed away. But it contains something more useful: the full picture of your capital position heading into the new financial year.

Things like your current ratio, your net debt against earnings, and what your retained earnings say to a lender are all sitting there already — they just don't usually get read through that lens.

If a bank conversation, a capital raise, or a new facility is on the horizon in FY27, have you looked at what the June 30 balance sheet will show?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
For business owners on the east coast preparing to close the financial year, the balance sheet sitting in your June 30 accounts contains more than a compliance record — it is the most complete picture of your business's capital position you will have all year.

Three things in that balance sheet are worth reading before it gets filed. Your current ratio tells you whether short-term obligations are covered by liquid assets or by future revenue. Your net debt to EBITDA ratio tells you how much additional borrowing capacity you carry into the new financial year. And your retained earnings balance tells any lender or investor something about the financial discipline inside the business.

With June 30 eleven days away, businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria are finalising accounts right now. Reading those accounts for capital signals — not just for the tax outcome — takes less time than most owners expect, and answers the questions that tend to come up in August when the starting position can no longer be changed.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led SMEs across the east coast to connect the EOFY accounts to the commercial decisions that follow. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
